skill.name: migration-generator
skill.description: Generate safe, reversible database migration plans and examples from schema changes across SQL databases and common ORM frameworks.
skill.summary: Generates reviewed database migration guidance for SQL, Prisma, TypeORM, Alembic, Django, Rails, and related workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, reconnaissance, SQLite, and Ruby backtick patterns, but review found these are documentation and migration examples rather than executable skill code. The main residual risk is that the skill includes shell commands and destructive SQL snippets that users may copy into real projects, so it should be published with a review warning rather than blocked.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Migration Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill includes command examples for Prisma, TypeORM, Alembic, and Django migrations. They are not executed by the skill, but users could copy them into projects where they may modify or reset databases if used without review. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for user-action risk, not malicious execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible bash examples for legitimate migration tools, including reset and rollback operations. I found no evidence that the skill executes them automatically, so the risk depends on user action.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Database Operation Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation contains DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, DELETE, and rollback examples. These are normal migration patterns, but incorrect use can destroy data in production. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk with legitimate intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive SQL is explicit and semantically related to database migrations. The surrounding text frames it as examples and safety guidance, not sabotage.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are caused by words such as password_hash, content_hash, and tree_hash in migration examples and generated metadata. No MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptographic implementation was found in the skill content. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines use hash as a field name or report metadata, not as a cryptographic algorithm. No evidence found of weak algorithm selection or credential hashing logic.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance hits map to SQL introspection and migration status examples, such as showmigrations and schema_migrations. They do not enumerate host, network, or credential data. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is database migration status checking and marketplace content, not system or network discovery. No evidence found of commands such as host enumeration or network scanning.

low_findings.title: SQLite Database Static Findings Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The SQLite hits are framework support references and Django migration imports. No embedded database file, credential store, or data dump appears in the reviewed files. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines refer to supported databases and Django migration modules. No evidence found of an actual SQLite database file or sensitive data payload.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Reset and Rollback Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include migration reset, rollback, and downgrade commands. They are legitimate for development and recovery, but can be destructive if copied into production without safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear, executable examples. The skill labels some as development or rollback usage, which reduces malicious concern but does not remove operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive SQL Migration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include dropping tables and columns and deleting migration records. These patterns are expected in migration guidance but require staging tests, backups, and human review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SQL statements are explicitly destructive, but they appear in standard migration examples and safety sections. No evidence found that they are hidden or automatically executed.

content.value_statement: Database schema changes can cause data loss, outages, or rollback failures. This skill helps create reviewed migration plans, rollback steps, and framework-specific examples.
actual_capabilities: Drafts forward and rollback migration plans for common schema changes.
Provides examples for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, Prisma, TypeORM, Alembic, Django, and Rails.
Suggests safer patterns for nullable columns, backfills, indexes, and foreign keys.
Outlines zero-downtime strategies for renames, removals, and required fields.
Creates pre-migration and post-migration review checklists.
Explains data migration approaches for large tables and batch updates.
limitations: It does not inspect a live database unless the user provides schema details.
It does not execute migrations or verify results automatically.
Generated migrations require review by a developer or database administrator.
Framework syntax may need adjustment for project-specific versions and conventions.

use_cases.title: Create ORM migrations
use_cases.description: Generate migration drafts for Prisma, TypeORM, Django, Alembic, Rails, and similar tools.

use_cases.title: Review schema changes
use_cases.description: Assess destructive operations, rollback paths, indexes, constraints, and data safety before deployment.

use_cases.title: Plan production releases
use_cases.description: Prepare staged migration plans, validation checks, and zero-downtime rollout steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Simple Column
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a safe migration to add an email_verified boolean column to users, including rollback and review notes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Related Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a migration plan for a user_profiles table with a one-to-one users relationship, indexes, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Backfill Existing Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a batched migration to split full_name into first_name and last_name for a large users table.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Zero Downtime Rename
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a zero-downtime migration plan to rename users.email to users.email_address with application deployment phases.

output_examples.input: Add a required phone column to users without downtime.
output: Adds the column as nullable first.
Backfills existing rows before enforcing the constraint.
Adds a rollback plan and validation checklist.
Calls out production risks before applying the change.

output_examples.input: Create a TypeORM migration for orders linked to users.
output: Creates an orders table with amount, status, timestamps, and user reference.
Adds indexes for common lookup paths.
Defines a foreign key and a matching rollback sequence.
Notes where project naming conventions may need adjustment.

output_examples.input: Prepare a large table backfill for user roles.
output: Splits the change into schema, backfill, and constraint phases.
Uses batches to reduce lock time and transaction size.
Keeps a clear rollback plan for each phase.

best_practices: Provide the current schema, target schema, framework, and database engine in the prompt.
Test every generated migration on local and staging databases before production use.
Review rollback paths, backups, lock behavior, and data loss risks before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated migrations directly against production without human review.
Do not combine unrelated schema changes and data backfills in one large migration.
Do not ignore framework version differences or project-specific migration conventions.

faq.question: Does this skill run migrations?
faq.answer: No. It generates migration guidance and examples. A user must review and run any commands separately.

faq.question: Which databases does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes examples for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and patterns used by common ORM tools.

faq.question: Can it create rollback steps?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to include down migrations, rollback notes, and safety checks where possible.

faq.question: Can it handle large table migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest batching, phased deploys, and validation steps for large data changes.

faq.question: Is generated SQL ready for production?
faq.answer: No. Treat it as a draft that needs review, testing, backups, and environment-specific changes.

faq.question: Does it inspect my existing database automatically?
faq.answer: No. Provide schema details, model changes, or migration tool output for better results.
